coredata.dc:title: Electrochemically generated green-fluorescent N-doped carbon quantum dots for facile monitoring alkaline phosphatase activity based on the Fe3+-mediating ON-OFF-ON-OFF fluorescence principle
coredata.dc:creator: Niu, Fushuang
Ying, Yi-Lun
Hua, Xin
Niu, Yusheng
Xu, Yuanhong
Long, Yi-Tao
coredata.dc:description: Herein, green-fluorescent N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with quantum yield of 30.6% were prepared via a simple bottom-up electrochemical (EC) method using the mixtures of pyrocatechol and ethylenediamine as both precursors and electrolytes. The ON-OFF fluorescence (FL) principle that the FL intensity of the N-CQDs was quenched in the presence of Fe3+ was further extended to the ON-OFF-ON and ON-OFF-ON-OFF ones for pyrophosphate anions (PPi) and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) detection, respectively, based on the specific competitive interaction of N-CQDs with Fe3+ and PPi and the hydrolysis of PPi in the presence ALP. Under the optimized conditions, limit of detection (LOD) of 1.2 μM and 0.5 μM were achieved for assays of Fe3+ and PPi, respectively. Meanwhile, measurable evaluation of ALP activity was reached in a wide range from 5 to 360 U/L with a LOD of 1.1 U/L. The as-proposed sensors showed many advantages including label free, rapid, simple, low-cost, high sensitive, which has great promising application potential in vivo and in vitro clinical diagnosis and will promote the application of EC derived carbon nanomaterials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical generation
Carbon quantum dots
Alkaline phosphatase activity
ON-OFF-ON-OFF principle

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity assessment N-CQDs or N-CQDs-Fe3+ based sensing for Fe3+ or PPi detection
Synthesis and characterization of the as-prepared N-CQDs
Principle of PPi and ALP level detection based on the Fe3+ mediating FL of the N-CQDs
PPi detection based on the ON-OFF-ON FL principle of N-CQDs
Monitoring the ALP activity based on the ON-OFF-ON-OFF FL principle of N-CQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science & Technology Fund Planning Project of Shandong Colleges and Universities

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Qingdao University of Science and Technology

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (No 21575071 ), Science & Technology Fund Planning Project of Shandong Colleges and Universities ( J16LA13 ), Qingdao Basic & Applied Research Project ( 15-9-1-100-jch ), Qingdao Science & Technology Planning Project ( 17-6-3-15-gx ) and Open Funds of the State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry ( SKLEAC201601 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the N-CQDs synthesis and the detection strategies for PPi and ALP activity based on the aggregation and disaggregation of the N-CQDs. WE and CE represent the working and counter electrodes, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Representative TEM image and HRTEM image (inset) of the as-prepared N-CQDs; (B) the particle size distribution of the N-CQDs obtained from TEM characterization. (C) AFM image and (D) the corresponding height profile along the white line in (C) for the as-prepared N-CQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
XPS spectra of the as-prepared N-CQDs: (A) survey XPS data; narrow scan spectra of C1s (B), O1s (C) and N1s (D), respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) UV–Vis absorption and FL spectra of N-CQDs. Photographs of the as-prepared N-CQDs suspensions under room light (inset, left) and under 365 nm UV light (inset, right), respectively. (B) Emission (Em) spectra at progressively increasing excitation (Ex) wavelengths from 300 nm to 450 nm of the N-CQDs suspension. (C) The emission colors of the as-prepared N-CQDs in 1931 CIE chromaticity diagram. (D) FL decay spectra and fitting curves of the as-obtained N-CQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Comparison of FL intensities change for N-CQDs upon addition of different metal ions all at the concentrations of 400 μM. I0 and I are the FL intensities of N-CQDs in the absence and presence of different metal ions, respectively. (B) FL emission spectra of the N-CQDs in the presence of Fe3+ ions at different concentrations. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The changes of FL spectra of N-CQDs in the presence of different composition: (a) blank; (b) Fe3+ (600 μM); (c) Fe3+ (600 μM) and PPi (1500 μM); and (d) Fe3+ (600 μM), PPi (1500 μM) and ALP (1000 U/L). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) The changes of FL spectra of N-CQDs-Fe3+ mixture with increasing concentrations of PPi under excitation wavelength of 410 nm. (B) Plots of FL intensity change (I − I0)/I0 vs. ALP concentration. (C) Linear plots of FL intensity change (I − I0)/I0 as a function of the ALP concentration. (D) Relative FL intensity (I − I0)/I0 of the sensing system in the presence of different anions. The concentration of each anion is 1500 μM. I0 and I are the FL intensities of N-CQDs-Fe3+ mixture in the absence and presence of PPi, respectively. Error bars indicate standard deviations of the means (n = 3). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) The FL intensity of the mixture suspension containing N-CQDs, 600 μM Fe3+, 1500 μM PPi and 1000 U/L ALP vs. the incubation time. (B) The changes of FL spectra of N-CQDs-Fe3+-PPi mixture upon addition with increasing concentrations of PPi with incubation of 60 min at 37 °C upon excitation at 410 nm. (C) FL intensity change (I − I0)/I0 of the resultant mixture vs. ALP concentration. Inset: the linear relationship between (I − I0)/I0 and ALP concentration. (D) The selectivity of ALP assays in the presence of GDH, GOx, thrombin, Asp, Cys, Ser, His, Met, Arg, Gal, ALP, respectively. The concentration of Gal, GDH, GOx, thrombin and ALP were all at 1000 U/L, six amino acids were all at 0.1 mM I0 and I are the FL intensities of N-CQDs-Fe3+-PPi mixture in the absence and presence of ALP, respectively. Error bars indicate standard deviations of the means (n = 3). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Electrochemically generated green-fluorescent N-doped carbon quantum dots for facile monitoring alkaline phosphatase activity based on the Fe3+-mediating ON-OFF-ON-OFF fluorescence principle

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Sciences, Qingdao University, Qingdao, 266071, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Advanced Materials and School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai, 200237, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Advanced Materials and School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
East China University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, green-fluorescent N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with quantum yield of 30.6% were prepared via a simple bottom-up electrochemical (EC) method using the mixtures of pyrocatechol and ethylenediamine as both precursors and electrolytes. The ON-OFF fluorescence (FL) principle that the FL intensity of the N-CQDs was quenched in the presence of Fe3+ was further extended to the ON-OFF-ON and ON-OFF-ON-OFF ones for pyrophosphate anions (PPi) and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) detection, respectively, based on the specific competitive interaction of N-CQDs with Fe3+ and PPi and the hydrolysis of PPi in the presence ALP. Under the optimized conditions, limit of detection (LOD) of 1.2 μM and 0.5 μM were achieved for assays of Fe3+ and PPi, respectively. Meanwhile, measurable evaluation of ALP activity was reached in a wide range from 5 to 360 U/L with a LOD of 1.1 U/L. The as-proposed sensors showed many advantages including label free, rapid, simple, low-cost, high sensitive, which has great promising application potential in vivo and in vitro clinical diagnosis and will promote the application of EC derived carbon nanomaterials.

N-CQDs were synthesized via a facile bottom-up electrochemical method for specific detection of PPi and ALP based on the Fe3+-mediating ON-OFF-ON(-OFF) fluorescent principle.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new class of carbon nanomaterials, have been attracting intense interest due to their excellent fluorescence (FL) properties, high chemical stability, low toxicity, anti-photobleaching, environmental friendliness, low cost and good biocompatibility [1–3]. All these unique and novel properties made them as new-generation fluorescent nanomaterials in optoelectronics, photocatalysis, bioimaging, chemical sensing, drug delivery, etc. [4–7] To date, various methods have been developed for the synthesis of CQDs such as laser ablation, microwave treatment, hydrothermal carbonization, electrochemical (EC) methods. Among them, the EC ones have satisfactory features for CQDs synthesis including simple operation, mild reaction conditions, low cost, mass production, and so on [8,9]. In addition, the bottom-up EC methods, which are employed for CQDs generation through electro-oxidation, electro-polymerization, carbonization and passivation from small molecular precursors including ethanol [10,11] and nitriles [12], amino acid [13], possess obvious superiorities of accurately regulating the morphology, size distribution, surface functionalization or heteroatom (e.g. N or/and S elements) doping of the CQDs [8,13]. It is a common phenomenon that the FL intensities of the EC-produced CQDs are significantly quenched in the presence of Fe3+. Based on this FL principle, the sensing applications of these CQDs can be used for but mostly limited in the specific Fe3+ assays till now [12,13]. Thus, it will greatly widen the applicability of the CQDs if Fe3+-mediating principle can be efficiently designed for sensing of more analytes such as anions or biological molecules [14].

Pyrophosphate anions (PPi), a kind of hydrolysis product of adenosine triphosphate, play important roles in various biological processes [15–17] such as cellular metabolism [17], enzymatic reactions [15], cancer research [18], etc. Accordingly, assay of PPi is of great significance in aqueous physiological system. Alkaline phosphatase (ALP), is one popularly used hydrolase enzymes, whose participation is vital in cell growth, apoptosis and signal transduction [19–22] as well as in diagnostics of cancers [23], liver dysfunction, diabetes [24] and bone diseases [24,25]. Thus, it is in great need to seek convenient and sensitive ways for monitoring ALP activity/level. Meanwhile, that PPi can be hydrolyzed by ALP shows great importance in vascular calcification and skeletal mineralization, and also make PPi as its natural substrate in the analysis of ALP [26]. Various strategies including colorimetry, surface enhanced Raman scattering, chromatographic analysis, fluorometric methods have been developed for detection of PPi or/and ALP activity [27–31]. Among them, fluorometric analysis is considered to be the more satisfying one because of its high sensitivity, convenience and simple instrument requirement, in which organic dyes [32,33], heavy-metal semiconductive quantum dots [34], and noble metal nanoparticles [26,35] are used as the fluorometric indicators. Despite of the advantages, these fluorophores usually suffer from some drawbacks including with poor photostability, complex synthesis procedure, high toxicity, or high cost [36], which greatly impedes their practical applications. Thus, it is still in urgent need to explore efficient fluorescent probes with good photostability, excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, simple preparation process and cost-effectiveness for fluorometric assays of PPi and ALP. Subsequently, CQDs synthesized via EC methods were suggested to be promising fluorescent alternatives, but have not been reported till now.

In this study, green-fluorescent N-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) were prepared via a simple bottom-up EC method using the mixtures of pyrocatechol and ethylenediamine as both precursors and electrolytes. The as-prepared N-CQDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), etc. The ON-OFF principle that the FL intensity of the N-CQDs was quenched in the presence of Fe3+ was further extended to the ON-OFF-ON and ON-OFF-ON-OFF modes for PPi and ALP detection, respectively, based on the turn-on and turn-off signal caused by disaggregation and reaggregation of N-CQDs. PPi thereinto acted as the substrate for ALP activity assessment simultaneously. Under the optimized conditions, sensitive and specific quantifications of Fe3+ and PPi and monitoring the ALP activity were achieved accordingly.

All the reagents were of analytical grade and used as received. Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) and pyrophosphate anions (PPi) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany). Ethylenediamine, pyrocatechol, sodium chloride (NaCl), disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), mono potassium phosphate (KH2PO4) were purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute (Tianjin, China). Dehydrogenase (GDH), galactosidase (Gal), glucose oxidase (GOx), thrombin, l-cysteine (Cys), l-aspartic acid (Asp), l-serine (Ser), l-methionine (Met), l-histidine (His), l-arginine (Arg) and the MD34 dialysis bag with cut-off molecular weight of 3500 Da were purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm−1) was obtained with a Milli-Q ultrapure system (Qingdao, China).

RXN-305D DC power supply was supplied by Shenzhen Zhaoxin Yuan Electronics Co., Ltd (Shenzhen, China). The FL and ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were measured on an Edinburgh instruments spectrofluorometer FS5 (Edinburgh, U. K.) and a Mapada UV-1800PC spectrophotometer (Shanghai, China), respectively. The TEM morphology of N-CQDs was observed on a JEOL Ltd JEM-2010 transmission electron microscope (JEOL Ltd., Japan). AFM images were obtained by using a SPI3800N microscope operating in the tapping mode (Seiko Instruments Inc., Japan). AFM samples were prepared by depositing the N-CQDs dispersions on freshly cleaved mica surfaces through a drop-casting method, respectively, and then were left to air-dry. FTIR were recorded on a Nicolet 5700 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Electron Scientific Instruments Corp., USA). XPS data were obtained on an ESCALab220i-XL electron spectrometer (VG Scientific, West Sussex, U. K.) using 300 W Al Kα radiation.

A facile EC system has been developed for the synthesis of N-CQDs (Fig. 1 ). Two Pt sheets (1.0 cm2) were employed as the working and auxiliary electrodes, respectively. The electrolytes were prepared by adding 0.11 g pyrocatechol in 10 mL ultrapure water containing 5 mM ethanediamine. A constant potential of 10 V was applied to advance the EC reaction using a DC power. The color of the electrolyte mixture changed from colorless to yellow and then to dark brown in 30 min. In order to purify the N-CQDs, the dark brown aqueous solution was subjected to dialysis against ultrapure water through a membrane (MWCO of 3500) for 24 h. The purified aqueous suspension of N-CQDs was diluted for 20 times with a phosphate buffer solution (PBS) (0.1 M, pH = 6.0) used for the following characterizations.

The QY of the N-CQDs were determined on the basis of previously established procedure. Typically, quinine sulfate (literature quantum yield 0.54) in 0.1 M H2SO4 aqueous solution was used as the reference [37,38]. In order to minimize the re-absorption effects, the aqueous suspension of N-CQDs and the aqueous solution of reference sample were diluted to keep the absorption intensity below 0.1 and 0.05, respectively, at the excitation wavelength of 375 nm. The QY of the N-CQDs was calculated using the equation below [39]:
Φ X
=
Φ
ST
(
Grad
X
Grad
ST
)
(
η X 2
η
ST
2
)
where the subscripts ST and X refer to quinine sulfate and N-CQDs, respectively, Φ represents the QY. Grad represents the gradient from the plot of integrated FL intensity vs. absorbance, and η is the refractive index of the solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity assessment N-CQDs or N-CQDs-Fe3+ based sensing for Fe3+ or PPi detection
ce:section.ce:para: Different metal ions (Ag+, Al3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Hg+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Fe3+) were chosen to assess the selectivity of the quenched FL of N-CQDs towards Fe3+. Each metal ion with final concentration of 400 μM was added and incubated for 3 min at room temperature before the spectral measurements. For the selectivity test to PPi, eight kinds of different anions including F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, NO3 − and Ac− were chosen to evaluate the influence of anions on the FL of N-CQDs-Fe3+ system. Each anion at 1500 μM was introduced into the above N-CQDs-Fe system and incubated for 3 min at room temperature. Tris-HCl buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4, 100 mM NaCl) was selected as the butter for selectivity assessment experiments. The FL spectra were recorded under excitation at 410 nm.

Firstly, to optimize the incubation time for quantitative assay of ALP activity, the FL intensities of N-CQDs solution containing Fe3+ (600 μM), PPi (1500 μM) added with ALP (1000 U/L) were incubated and monitored by every 10 min for 70 min at the excitation wavelength of 410 nm. For the enzyme assay, different levels of ALP with different activity ranging from 5 to 900 U/L was respectively added into the N-CQDs suspension containing Fe3+ (600 μM), PPi (1500 μM). After the optimum incubation time, the FL spectra of the mixtures were recorded, respectively. To explore the specificity of the as-proposed method, the N-CQDs-Fe3+-PPi system was treated with several other enzymes including GDH, Gal, GOx, thrombin, and different kinds of amino acids (Cys, Asp, Ser, Met, His, Arg) under the same conditions as ALP. The corresponding FL intensity was recorded under excitation at 410 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the as-prepared N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1, a two-electrode EC system has been applied for the synthesis of N-CQDs. Two Pt sheets (1.0 cm2) were employed as the working and auxiliary electrodes, respectively. 0.1 M pyrocatechol aqueous solution containing 5 mM ethanediamine was used as the electrolyte. When a constant potential of 10 V was applied, the mixture of pyrocatechol and ethanediamine underwent electro-oxidation electro-polymerization, carbonization, and passivation processes to form the N-CQDs suspension. The color of the electrolyte solution changed from colorless to yellow and then to orange and brown, and finally to dark brown. After dialysis, the as-prepared suspension was then diluted to colorless or light yellow, which showed strong green emission under the 365 nm UV irradiation. The EC oxidation process can endow the surface of the N-CQDs with a great deal of functional oxygenous groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl ones, which endow the as-prepared N-CQDs with well aqueous dispersibility. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the typical TEM image reveals that the N-CQDs can be well mono-dispersed in aqueous medium with uniform spherical morphology. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (inset in Fig. 2A) shows an in-plane lattice spacing of 0.210 nm in the crystalline structures of the N-CQDs, which matches well with the (102) diffraction planes of sp2 graphitic carbon and is close to the (103) diffraction plane of diamond-like (sp3) carbon [40]. The as-prepared N-CQDs were with average lateral diameter of 3.15 ± 0.12 nm and a narrow size distribution as indicated in Fig. 2B, which were similar to the previously reported CQDs [41]. The AFM characterization was further carried out to give the thickness of the as-prepared N-CQDs, which again confirmed that the as-obtained N-CQDs were homogeneously dispersed with height ranging from 1 to 2.5 nm (Fig. 2C and D).

In order to investigate the composition and functional groups of the as-prepared N-CQDs, XPS characterization was further carried out (Fig. 3 ). There are three significant peaks at 284.8 eV, 400.0 eV and 532.6 eV in the survey spectra, corresponding to elemental species C, N and O, respectively (Fig. 3A). The C1s spectrum reveals four obvious peaks at 284.6, 285.2, 286.3 and 288.3 eV, which are ascribed to the bonds of CC, CN, CO and CO, respectively (Fig. 3B). The peaks at 399.7 eV and 400.6 eV should be attributed to CN and NH (Fig. 3C), respectively. The deconvolution of the O1s spectrum verify the existence of CO (531.8 eV) and CO (533.0 eV), respectively (Fig. 3D) [42–44]. These results indicated that the as-prepared N-CQDs possess predominant functional groups including with hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxylic and amine groups. FTIR spectroscopy was also employed to further investigate the functional groups on the surface of the as-prepared N-CQDs (Fig. S1). The results in detail for N-CQDs is presented in the Supporting Information, which again confirmed the presence of the functional groups such as CO (1633.5 cm−1), CO (1108 cm−1), NH (3290.2 cm−1) on the surface of the N-CQDs. All these functional groups are favorable for the good dispersibility of the N-CQDs in the aqueous medium [45–48].

In order to study the optical properties of the N-CQDs, the UV–vis and FL spectra were collected. The inset in Fig. 4 A displays the photographs of the N-CQDs suspensions, which were obtained under room light (inset, left) and UV light (365 nm), respectively. As shown, strong green FL were observed for the as-prepared N-CQDs suspensions upon the 365 nm UV light irradiation. The UV–vis spectra shows a maximum absorption peak at 410 nm, which is consistent with the value of the maximum excitation wavelength, under which highest emission peak at 518 nm was obtained (Fig. 4A). Fig. 4B illustrates the typical FL spectra of N-CQDs with the excitation wavelength increasing from 300 to 450 nm. The positions of the emission peaks were scarcely shifted with the changing excitation wavelength, which suggests the good monodispersion of as-prepared N-CQDs in size and FL, and provides a distinguishing superiority to other CQDs with λex-dependent emission behaviors for analytical application [50]. As shown in Fig. 4C, the distinct green emission of the N-CQDs was further confirmed in the 1931 CIE chromaticity diagram. The QY of the N-CQDs was measured to be 30.6% using quinine sulfate as reference. The high QY should be attributed to the nitrogen-dopment in the as-prepared N-CQDs. The lifetime of the N-CQDs was also measured. As indicated, the FL decay is mono-exponential with lifetimes of 3.71 ns (Fig. 4D), which shows similar FL property to CQDs reported previously [49]. The effect of pH of the background buffer on the FL intensity of the N-CQDs was investigated in consideration of the fact that environments with various pH are often encountered in practical applications. As shown in Fig. S2, the FL intensity of the N-CQDs was slightly influenced by varying pH, which is much favorable for their further practical applications since different pH environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of PPi and ALP level detection based on the Fe3+ mediating FL of the N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: It is generally known that some metal ions can interact with CQDs through strong complexation between the metal ion and the oxygen-containing groups on the surface of the CQDs. Thus, the influences of metal ions all at 400 μM including Ag+, Al3+, Cd2+, Co2+, Hg+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Fe3+ on the FL intensity of N-CQDs were studied, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5 A, introduction of Fe3+ brings significantly quenching impact to the FL intensity and slightly blue shifting of the emission maximum of N-CQDs, whereas other metal ions showed negligible or no influence on the FL properties of the N-CQDs. It was speculated that due to the strong affinity interactions between Fe3+ and the N-CQDs, presence of Fe3+ would make N-CQDs aggregate to larger particles resulting in the turn-off FL signal intensity and blue-shift emission maximum position of the N-CQDs [51]. As shown in Fig. 5B, the FL intensity of the N-CQDs gradually diminished with the continuous addition of Fe3+. Based on this ON-OFF principle, the specific fluorescent determination of Fe3+ was usually achieved (Fig. S4 in the Supporting Information), which was consistent with many previous reports that using CQDs for Fe3+ assays. Almost complete quenching of the N-CQDs FL was observed when 600 μM of Fe3+ was added, which was then selected for further experiment.

As shown in Figs. 1 and 6 , it was found that addition of PPi into the N-CQDs suspension containing 600 μM Fe3+ could lead to significant FL recovery, while further addition of ALP would again lead to the quenching of the FL intensity of the N-CQDs. It is reported that Fe3+ preferably generates stable complexes with PPi [52]. Due to the stronger affinity of Fe3+ to PPi than to the oxygenous groups on the N-CQDs, Fe3+ is released from the N-CQDs-Fe3+ aggregates, leading to the FL recovery or enhancement of the N-CQDs accordingly (signal-on process). As PPi can be substrate of ALP, it is expected that when ALP is added to the N-CQDs-Fe3+-PPi system, PPi can be hydrolyzed by ALP to disintegrate the PPi–Fe3+ complexes. The free Fe3+ could re-aggregate the N-CQDs, resulting in the FL quenching of the N-CQDs again (signal-off process). As a control, it was found that pure PPi or ALP brought negligible influence on the FL intensity of N-CQDs (Fig. S3). As a result, strategies for PPi and ALP assays can be designed based on signal-on and signal-off processes caused by the aggregation and disaggregation of N-CQDs in the presence of different compositions of Fe3+, PPi or ALP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PPi detection based on the ON-OFF-ON FL principle of N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Detection of PPi was further carried out based on the ON–OFF–ON FL of the N-CQDs (Fig. 1). As shown in Fig. 7 A, continuous addition of PPi into the N-CQDs suspension containing 600 μM Fe3+ (N-CQDs-Fe3+) could lead to gradual FL recovery of the N-CQDs-Fe3+ system recorded at excitation wavelength of 410 nm. The best FL recovery ratio is up to 81% upon addition of PPi at the concentration of 1500 μM. As shown in Fig. 7B, the change of the FL intensity ((I I0)/I0, Y) showed good linear relationship against the PPi concentration (C) ranging from 5 to 500 μM. This equation can be expressed as Y = 0.0096 X + 0.1375 with R2 = 0.996 (Fig. 7C). The limit of detection (LOD) for PPi was determined to be 0.5 μM (S/N ≥ 3). To investigate the selectivity of this FL sensor, different kinds of other anions including F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, Ac− and PO4 3− all at the concentration of 1500 μM were separately added into the aqueous N-CQDs-Fe3+ dispersion and the FL response was recorded correspondingly. As illustrated in Fig. 7D, the addition of F−, Cl−, Br−, I− SO4 2−, NO3 −, PO4 3 − and Ac− had a slight influence on the FL intensity of the N-CQDs-Fe3+ system. The presence of PO4 3− led to a visible FL enhancement due to its ability to form Fe3+-PO4 3- complex. However, the intensity change ratio of (I I0)/I0 is up to 8.7 upon addition with PPi while it was 2 for that with PO4 3−. Accordingly, the influence of PO4 3− can be neglected compared with the stronger FL increase induced by PPi. These results confirmed the good selectivity of the N-CQDs-Fe3+ based FL strategy for PPi detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring the ALP activity based on the ON-OFF-ON-OFF FL principle of N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As indicated above, the FL intensity of N-CQDs-Fe3+ reached highest when PPi at concentration of 1500 μM was added, above which negligible FL change was observed with PPi. Thus, the concentration of 1500 μM PPi was selected for further ALP assays. The activity of ALP was studied by fluorometric method based on the N-CQDs aqueous solution containing Fe3+ and PPi as the background assay solution. As shown in Fig. 8 A, the typical FL spectra were consecutively recorded every 10 min after the addition of 1000 U/L ALP. Gradually increased FL quenching was observed due to catalysis activity of ALP for PPi hydrolysis to release more free Fe3+ with the prolonged incubation time. The dependence of FL quenching degree on the incubation time became much smaller after 60 min. Considering the best compromise of the enhancement effect and time consumption, 60 min was chosen as the optimal incubation time for quantitative assay of ALP activity. The reactions were monitored by recording the FL emission intensities upon addition with different units (5–900 U/L) of ALP activity (Fig. 8B). The FL intensity of the resultant mixture decreased at different rates with the different added ALP concentrations (Fig. 8C). It was obtained that the relative FL ((I0 − I)/I, Y) was linear with the ALP concentration (X) in the range of 5–360 U/L. The fitting equation can be expressed as Y = 0.00149 X + 0.00539 with R2 = 0.997. The LOD is estimated as 1.1 U/L (S/N ≥ 3) for ALP. Thus, the as-proposed assay is capable of practical detection of ALP level in human body, because the normal value for which was reported to be 40–190 U/L in blood [45]. Furthermore, the interference experiments indicated the tested enzymes and amino acids have negligible effect on the FL intensity of N-CQDs-Fe3+-PPi system (Fig. 8D), again confirming the excellent selectivity of the developed FL system for ALP sensing.

In summary, green-fluorescent N-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) were prepared via a simple bottom-up EC method using the mixtures of pyrocatechol and ethylenediamine as both precursors and electrolytes. Based on the competitive interaction of N-CQDs with Fe3+ and PPi and the hydrolysis of PPi in the presence ALP, PPi and ALP assays can be achieved based on the ON-OFF-ON and ON-OFF-ON-OFF FL principle derived from the ON-OFF one that of the FL intensity of the N-CQDs was quenched in the presence of Fe3+. Under the optimized conditions, LODs of Fe3+ and PPi with 1.2 μM and 0.5 μM were achieved. Meanwhile, ALP activity was measured to be in a broad range from 5 to 360 U/L with a LOD of 1.1 U/L, which is sensitive enough for practical determination. The as-proposed sensors showed many advantages including rapidity, simplicity, low cost, high sensitivity, which has great promising application potential in vivo and in vitro clinical diagnosis and promote the application of carbon-based nanomaterials in biosensors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (No 21575071), Science & Technology Fund Planning Project of Shandong Colleges and Universities (J16LA13), Qingdao Basic & Applied Research Project (15-9-1-100-jch), Qingdao Science & Technology Planning Project (17-6-3-15-gx) and Open Funds of the State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry (SKLEAC201601).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
mmc1 mmc1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2017.10.097.
